seo.title: Cybersecurity Voice Agents AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for voice agents AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying voice agents in cybersecurity operations introduces unique risks where real-time speech pipelines can inadvertently expose classified threat intelligence, trigger unauthorized automated responses, or be manipulated through adversarial audio inputs. This checklist ensures your voice-driven security operations center (SOC) agents meet NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 requirements while maintaining sub-second latency without sacrificing safety controls. Given that failure consequences include adversary exploitation and operational outages, every deployment decision must be treated as mission-critical.

sections.heading: Data Protection & Threat Intelligence Containment
sections.description: Prevent threat intelligence, IOCs, and classified security data from leaking through LLM APIs or voice transcription pipelines to unauthorized parties or adversaries.

items.label: Sanitize STT Output Before LLM Ingestion
items.description: Implement a real-time redaction layer between speech-to-text output and LLM input that strips CVE identifiers, IP addresses, hash values, and classified threat actor TTPs before sending to any external LLM API endpoint.

items.label: Enforce LLM API Data Residency Constraints
items.description: Confirm that all LLM API providers used in the voice pipeline are contractually prohibited from training on submitted data and that data residency is restricted to approved jurisdictions per your ISO 27001 Statement of Applicability.

items.label: Classify Voice Session Transcripts as Sensitive Assets
items.description: Tag all STT transcripts, LLM prompts, and TTS outputs generated during security operations as Confidential or higher in your data classification schema, applying encryption at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.3).

items.label: Implement Prompt Injection Detection for Voice Input
items.description: Deploy an audio and text-layer filter that detects adversarial prompt injection attempts embedded in voice inputs, including attempts to override system instructions or extract internal threat intelligence via social engineering phrases.

items.label: Restrict LLM Context Window to Need-to-Know Data
items.description: Configure the LLM system prompt and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline to only inject threat intelligence relevant to the active incident, preventing the model from accessing or surfacing unrelated classified IOC databases.

items.label: Audit All External API Calls from Voice Pipeline
items.description: Log every outbound API call made by the LLM reasoning layer, including request payloads (redacted), response metadata, and timestamps, to a SIEM-integrated audit trail that is immutable and retained for a minimum of 12 months per SOC 2 CC7.2.

items.label: Test for Data Exfiltration via TTS Output
items.description: Conduct quarterly red team exercises specifically targeting the TTS output layer to verify that the voice agent cannot be coerced into reading out sensitive threat data, credentials, or classified advisories to unauthorized listeners.

sections.heading: Human-in-the-Loop Controls for Automated Response Actions
sections.description: Ensure that AI-driven voice agents cannot autonomously execute critical security response actions without verified human approval, preventing false positives from causing outages or escalating incidents.

items.label: Define and Enforce a Tiered Action Authorization Matrix
items.description: Create a documented matrix classifying all possible agent-triggered actions (e.g., firewall rule changes, endpoint isolation, account lockouts) into tiers: Tier 1 (autonomous allowed), Tier 2 (supervisor voice confirmation required), Tier 3 (out-of-band written approval required). Enforce this matrix programmatically in the agent orchestration layer.

items.label: Implement Voice-Confirmed Human Approval for Critical Actions
items.description: For any Tier 2 or Tier 3 action, require the agent to verbally read back the proposed action, affected system identifiers, and expected impact, then capture an explicit spoken confirmation from an authenticated SOC analyst before execution.

items.label: Authenticate Approving Analyst via Voice Biometrics or MFA
items.description: Verify the identity of the human approver using voice biometric matching against enrolled profiles or require a concurrent MFA push notification to prevent spoofed approvals via recorded audio or impersonation.

items.label: Enforce a Mandatory Cooling-Off Period for Cascading Actions
items.description: Program the agent to enforce a configurable delay (minimum 30 seconds) between sequential automated actions affecting the same system or network segment, preventing runaway automation chains that could escalate an incident.

items.label: Provide a Universal Voice-Activated Kill Switch
items.description: Implement a hardcoded voice command (e.g., 'Abort all actions') that immediately halts all pending and queued agent-initiated actions and triggers an alert to the SOC lead, bypassing all LLM reasoning to ensure sub-100ms response.

items.label: Log Human Approval Decisions with Full Context
items.description: Record every human approval or rejection of an agent-proposed action, including the analyst's identity, the verbatim voice transcript, the action parameters, and the outcome, in an immutable audit log linked to the incident ticket.

items.label: Simulate False Positive Scenarios in Tabletop Exercises
items.description: Conduct bi-annual tabletop exercises specifically simulating false positive detections where the voice agent proposes incorrect critical actions, validating that human-in-the-loop controls successfully prevent execution and that analysts are trained to challenge agent recommendations.

sections.heading: Voice Pipeline Latency, Accuracy & Safety Filtering
sections.description: Maintain sub-second latency SLAs while ensuring safety filters, CVE verification, and conversation coherence checks do not introduce unacceptable delays or degrade the accuracy of security advisories.

items.label: Establish and Monitor Latency Budgets per Pipeline Stage
items.description: Define maximum latency budgets for each stage: STT ≤150ms, safety filter ≤50ms, LLM inference ≤500ms, CVE verification lookup ≤100ms, TTS ≤150ms. Instrument each stage with real-time telemetry and alert when any stage exceeds its budget by more than 20%.

items.label: Verify AI-Generated Security Advisories Against Live CVE Databases
items.description: Before the TTS layer vocalizes any CVE reference, CVSS score, or remediation guidance, programmatically query NVD and MITRE CVE APIs to validate accuracy. Reject and regenerate any advisory where the agent's output conflicts with the authoritative CVE record.

items.label: Deploy a Real-Time Safety Filter for Harmful Security Content
items.description: Implement a dedicated safety classifier between the LLM output and TTS input that blocks the agent from vocalizing exploit code, active attack instructions, or unverified threat actor attribution that could cause harm if overheard or recorded.

items.label: Implement Conversation Coherence Validation Across Turns
items.description: Deploy a stateful coherence checker that flags when the agent's response in a multi-turn conversation contradicts a prior statement within the same session (e.g., changing a severity rating without new evidence), triggering a clarification prompt rather than proceeding.

items.label: Test STT Accuracy on Security-Specific Vocabulary
items.description: Benchmark your STT provider's word error rate (WER) specifically on a security domain vocabulary corpus including CVE IDs, MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs, vendor names, and technical jargon. Require WER ≤3% before production deployment.

items.label: Implement Graceful Degradation When Latency Budgets Are Breached
items.description: Define a fallback behavior for when cumulative pipeline latency exceeds 1 second: the agent must vocalize a holding message, queue the response, and complete processing rather than timing out silently or providing a truncated unsafe response.

items.label: Conduct Adversarial Audio Input Testing
items.description: Perform quarterly adversarial testing using ultrasonic commands, audio adversarial examples, and background noise injection to verify that the STT and safety layers correctly reject or flag manipulated audio inputs that could alter agent behavior.

sections.heading: Compliance Governance, Audit Readiness & Incident Response
sections.description: Establish the governance structures, audit trails, and incident response procedures required to demonstrate continuous compliance with NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and CISA directives for AI voice agent deployments.

items.label: Map Voice Agent Controls to NIST CSF Functions
items.description: Document how each voice agent control maps to NIST CSF Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover functions. Maintain this mapping in your GRC platform and review it quarterly or after any significant architecture change.

items.label: Include Voice Agent Pipeline in SOC 2 Type II Scope
items.description: Explicitly include the STT provider, LLM API, TTS provider, and orchestration layer in your SOC 2 Type II system description and ensure your auditor tests controls covering availability, confidentiality, and processing integrity for the full pipeline.

items.label: Establish a Voice Agent-Specific Incident Response Playbook
items.description: Create a dedicated IR playbook for scenarios where the voice agent is compromised, produces harmful output, or triggers an unauthorized action. Define escalation paths, containment steps (including pipeline shutdown), evidence preservation procedures, and CISA notification timelines.

items.label: Conduct ISO 27001 Risk Assessment for Voice Agent Threat Vectors
items.description: Perform a formal ISO 27001 Annex A risk assessment specifically covering voice agent threat vectors: audio spoofing, LLM prompt injection, API key compromise, TTS output interception, and adversarial manipulation of threat intelligence context.

items.label: Implement Continuous Compliance Monitoring for CISA Directives
items.description: Subscribe to CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog updates and configure automated alerts when any software component in the voice agent pipeline (STT libraries, LLM SDKs, TTS engines) appears in the KEV catalog, triggering a 72-hour remediation SLA.

items.label: Maintain Immutable Voice Session Audit Logs for 12+ Months
items.description: Store all voice session logs, including STT transcripts, LLM reasoning traces, human approval records, and action execution logs, in a WORM-compliant storage system with cryptographic integrity verification, retained for a minimum of 12 months per SOC 2 and ISO 27001 requirements.

items.label: Perform Annual Third-Party Penetration Testing of Voice Pipeline
items.description: Engage a CREST-certified penetration testing firm annually to conduct a full-scope assessment of the voice agent pipeline, specifically targeting audio injection, API security, LLM jailbreaking, and unauthorized action execution vectors.

items.label: Train SOC Analysts on Voice Agent Limitations and Override Procedures
items.description: Deliver mandatory annual training to all SOC personnel covering the voice agent's known failure modes, how to recognize AI hallucinations in security advisories, how to invoke the kill switch, and how to escalate suspected agent compromise incidents.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: NIST CSF 2.0 Govern Function requires organizations to establish AI-specific risk governance policies. Voice agents used in security operations must have documented roles, responsibilities, and risk tolerances explicitly covering AI-assisted decision-making, and these policies must be reviewed annually or after any material change to the agent architecture.

SOC 2 Type II Processing Integrity criteria (PI1.1–PI1.5) require that system processing is complete, valid, accurate, timely, and authorized. For voice agents, this means your auditor will expect evidence that CVE verification checks, human approval workflows, and latency monitoring controls operated effectively across the entire audit period, not just at point-in-time assessments.

ISO 27001:2022 Annex A Control 8.28 (Secure Coding) and Control 5.23 (Information Security for Use of Cloud Services) directly apply to LLM API integrations in voice pipelines. Organizations must document supplier security assessments for all STT, LLM, and TTS providers and include AI API usage in their cloud security policy with explicit data handling requirements.

CISA's Binding Operational Directives (BODs) and Emergency Directives require federal agencies and critical infrastructure operators to remediate known exploited vulnerabilities within defined timeframes. Any voice agent pipeline component appearing in the CISA KEV catalog must be patched or mitigated within the directive's timeline, and organizations should maintain a software bill of materials (SBOM) for all pipeline dependencies to enable rapid identification and response.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your cybersecurity voice agent pipeline against NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 controls, automatically flagging compliance gaps before your next audit or incident.
